questions.question:
Suppose A = {a,b,c,d} and H_1 = {{a,b},{c},{d}} is the following partition of A.
a. List the ordered pairs of the equivalence relations induced by H_1.
b. Draw the graph of the above equivalence relation.
c. Let H_2 = {{a,b},{c,d}} and H_3 = {{a,b,c,d}}. Draw a Poset diagram of the poset ({H_1, H_2, H_3}, refines).
questions.answer: a. Equivalence relation E_1 = {(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(a,b),(b,a)}. b. Graph has nodes a,b,c,d with edges between a-b (same block) and self-loops on each node. c. Hasse diagram: H_1 is at the bottom, H_2 is above H_1 (H_1 refines H_2), H_3 is at the top (H_2 refines H_3), forming a chain H_1 < H_2 < H_3.
questions.to_find: Equivalence relation pairs, graph, and Poset diagram for the given partitions

understand.plain: A partition of A induces an equivalence relation: two elements are related iff they belong to the same block. The finer a partition is (more, smaller blocks), the lower it sits in the refinement order. H_1 has 3 blocks, H_2 has 2 blocks, H_3 has 1 block, so H_1 refines H_2 refines H_3.

keywords.explain: Given a partition, define x ~ y iff x and y are in the same block. This always gives a valid equivalence relation.
keywords.example: H_1 = {{a,b},{c},{d}} gives a~b and nothing else non-trivially, plus reflexive pairs.

keywords.explain: Partition P refines Q if every block of P is a subset of some block of Q. Finer partitions (more blocks) are smaller in the order.
keywords.example: {{a,b},{c},{d}} refines {{a,b},{c,d}} because {a,b} subset {a,b} and {c} subset {c,d} and {d} subset {c,d}.

keywords.explain: A Hasse diagram draws a partial order by placing smaller elements lower and connecting elements only when one directly covers the other.
keywords.example: H_1 at bottom, H_2 in middle, H_3 at top, with edges H_1->H_2 and H_2->H_3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='280' class='bg' rx='10'/><line x1='150' y1='60' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='150' y1='160' x2='150' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='40' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>H_3</text><circle cx='150' cy='140' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>H_2</text><circle cx='150' cy='240' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>H_1</text><text x='150' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Refinement Poset (H_1 < H_2 < H_3)</text><text x='190' y='45' class='sub mut'>{abcd}</text><text x='190' y='145' class='sub mut'>{ab},{cd}</text><text x='190' y='245' class='sub mut'>{ab},{c},{d}</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A Hasse diagram showing the chain H_1 (bottom) refines H_2 (middle) refines H_3 (top), where each partition is shown with its blocks.

given.aim: Find the equivalence relation induced by partition H_1 and draw the refinement Poset for {H_1, H_2, H_3}.

terms.meaning: The base set with four elements.
terms.example: Any partition of A splits these 4 elements into non-empty disjoint blocks.
terms.connects: The three partitions H_1, H_2, H_3 are all partitions of A.

terms.meaning: A partition with 3 blocks: one containing a and b, and two singleton blocks.
terms.example: Elements a and b are equivalent; c and d are each alone.
terms.connects: H_1 is the finest partition, so it is the smallest element in the refinement order.

terms.meaning: P refines Q (P <= Q) iff every block of P is contained in some block of Q.
terms.example: H_1 refines H_2: {a,b} subset {a,b}, {c} subset {c,d}, {d} subset {c,d}.
terms.connects: This defines the partial order on the set of partitions.

terms.meaning: The set of all pairs (x,y) such that x and y lie in the same block of H_1.
terms.example: E_1 = {(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(a,b),(b,a)} since only a,b share a block.
terms.connects: Every partition induces a unique equivalence relation, and vice versa.

given.plan: List all pairs (x,y) where x and y are in the same block of H_1 to get the equivalence relation. Then check refinement between all pairs of partitions to build the Hasse diagram.

steps.title: List ordered pairs of the equivalence relation induced by H_1
steps.formula_raw: x ~ y iff x and y are in the same block of the partition
steps.apply: Block {a,b}: pairs (a,a),(b,b),(a,b),(b,a). Block {c}: pair (c,c). Block {d}: pair (d,d). So E_1 = {(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(a,b),(b,a)}.
steps.note: Every equivalence relation from a partition includes all reflexive pairs plus symmetric pairs for elements sharing a block.

steps.title: Check refinement relationships between H_1, H_2, H_3
steps.formula_raw: P refines Q iff every block of P is a subset of some block of Q
steps.apply: H_1 refines H_2: {a,b}={a,b}, {c} subset {c,d}, {d} subset {c,d}. Yes. H_2 refines H_3: {a,b} subset {a,b,c,d}, {c,d} subset {a,b,c,d}. Yes. H_1 refines H_3 (transitively). So the order is H_1 < H_2 < H_3 (a chain).
steps.note: The discrete partition (all singletons) is always the minimum; the indiscrete partition (one block) is always the maximum.

steps.title: Draw Hasse diagram of ({H_1, H_2, H_3}, refines)
steps.formula_raw: Draw edge from x to y only when y covers x (x < y with nothing strictly between)
steps.apply: H_2 covers H_1 (no partition strictly between them), H_3 covers H_2. Hasse diagram: H_3 at top, H_2 in middle, H_1 at bottom, with edges H_1-H_2 and H_2-H_3.
steps.note: Since all three form a chain (total order), the Hasse diagram is a straight vertical line.

solution.result: Equivalence relation E_1 = {(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(a,b),(b,a)}. Refinement Poset: H_1 < H_2 < H_3 (a chain with H_1 at the bottom).

questions.formula_note: The partition-refinement order uses the partial order axioms; the Hasse cover relation determines which edges to draw in the diagram.

questions.question:
Let (S, <=) be a partial order with two minimal elements a and b, and a maximum element c. Let P: S -> {True, False} be a predicate defined on S. Suppose that P(a) = True, P(b) = False and P(x) => P(y) for all x <= y, where => stands for logical implication. Which of the following statements CANNOT be true?

A. P(x) = True for all x in S such that x >= b
B. P(x) = False for all x in S such that b >= x and x >= c
C. P(x) = False for all x in S such that not(x >= b) and not(b >= x)
D. P(x) = False for all x in S such that a <= x and b <= x
questions.answer: D. P(x) = False for all x in S such that a <= x and b <= x
questions.to_find: Which statement about predicate P on the partial order CANNOT be true

understand.plain: The predicate P is monotone: if x <= y then P(x) implies P(y). Since P(a) = True and a <= c, we must have P(c) = True. If a <= x and b <= x (i.e., x is an upper bound of both a and b), then both a <= x and since P(a) = True, monotonicity forces P(x) = True. So P(x) = False for elements above both a and b is impossible.

keywords.explain: An element m is minimal if no element is strictly below it; there can be multiple minimal elements in a partial order (unlike a minimum).
keywords.example: Here a and b are both minimal but neither is below the other.

keywords.explain: A predicate P is order-monotone if x <= y implies P(x) => P(y). Once True, it stays True as you go up.
keywords.example: P(a) = True and a <= c means P(c) must also be True.

keywords.explain: If x >= a and x >= b, then x is above both minimal elements, and since P(a) = True, monotonicity forces P(x) = True.
keywords.example: c is the maximum, so P(c) = True. Any element above a must have P = True.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='240' class='bg' rx='10'/><line x1='100' y1='170' x2='160' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='220' y1='170' x2='160' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='100' cy='185' r='22' class='ok'/><text x='100' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' fill='white'>a</text><circle cx='220' cy='185' r='22' class='no'/><text x='220' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' fill='white'>b</text><circle cx='160' cy='60' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text><text x='100' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P=True</text><text x='220' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P=False</text><text x='160' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P=True (forced)</text><text x='160' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut ink'>max c</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram with two minimal elements a (P=True) and b (P=False) at the bottom and maximum element c at the top; monotonicity forces P(c) = True.

given.aim: Find which statement about P cannot hold given monotonicity, P(a)=True, P(b)=False.

terms.meaning: A partial order set with two minimal elements a, b and a maximum element c.
terms.example: Think of a diamond-like Hasse diagram with a, b at the bottom and c at the top.
terms.connects: The structure allows elements that are comparable or incomparable.

terms.meaning: The predicate P is monotone: once True at some element, it is True at all elements above it.
terms.example: Since P(a) = True and a <= c, we get P(c) = True.
terms.connects: This constraint is the key to ruling out certain statements.

terms.term: Elements above both a and b
terms.meaning: Any element x with a <= x AND b <= x is an upper bound of both a and b.
terms.example: The maximum c satisfies a <= c and b <= c. If any x has a <= x, then P(x) = True by monotonicity.
terms.connects: Option D asks about such elements; since a <= x implies P(x)=True, P(x)=False is impossible.

given.plan: Test each option: use the monotonicity constraint P(x)=>P(y) for x<=y, together with P(a)=True and P(b)=False, to check if the statement leads to a contradiction.

steps.title: Establish forced truth values
steps.formula_raw: If x <= y and P(x) = True then P(y) = True (monotonicity)
steps.apply: P(a) = True. Since a is minimal but can have elements above it, for any x with a <= x, P(x) must be True. P(b) = False. For any y with y <= b, we cannot derive P(y) = True from this alone. But P(c) must be True because a <= c.
steps.note: The maximum c is above every element, so P(c) = P(a via chain) = True.

steps.title: Analyze option D
steps.formula_raw: D: P(x) = False for all x in S such that a <= x and b <= x
steps.apply: If a <= x, monotonicity gives P(a) => P(x), i.e., True => P(x), so P(x) = True. If additionally b <= x, that does not change P(x) = True. So no element that is above a can have P(x) = False. Option D requires P(x) = False for all elements above both a and b — but a <= x already forces P(x) = True. Contradiction.
steps.note: Option D CANNOT be true. Options A, B, C can potentially be consistent with the constraints.

solution.result: D. P(x) = False for all x in S such that a <= x and b <= x — this CANNOT be true because a <= x forces P(x) = True by monotonicity.

questions.formula_note: The monotonicity of P over the partial order is the key: partial-order-axioms combined with the implication P(x)=>P(y) for x<=y directly rules out option D.

questions.to_find: The set whose addition makes S a complete lattice under set containment

understand.plain: A complete lattice requires every pair of elements to have a least upper bound (join = union) and a greatest lower bound (meet = intersection) that also lie in the set. We check all pairs in S for their intersections; if any intersection is missing from S, we need to add it. The intersection of {1,3,5} and {1,2,4} is {1}, but checking {1,3,5} and {1,2,3} gives {1,3}, which is not in S — adding {1,3} fixes all missing meets.

keywords.explain: Every subset (including pairs) must have a join (lub) and meet (glb) within the set. For sets ordered by containment, join = union and meet = intersection.
keywords.example: If {A,B} are in the lattice, A union B and A intersection B must also be in the lattice.

keywords.explain: The meet of two sets A and B under containment order is their intersection A intersection B.
keywords.example: {1,3,5} meet {1,2,3} = {1,3}. If {1,3} is not in S, S is not a lattice.

keywords.explain: The join of two sets A and B under containment order is their union A union B.
keywords.example: {1,2} join {1,3,5} = {1,2,3,5}, but this may not need to be in S if a smaller ub in S covers it.

understand.visual_alt: Sets in S ordered by containment; checking pairwise intersections reveals {1,3} = {1,3,5} intersect {1,2,3} is missing from S.

given.aim: Find which additional set makes S a complete lattice under set containment (subset ordering).

terms.example: The top element is {1,2,3,4,5} (contains all others). {1,2} is a minimal element.
terms.connects: We need every pairwise meet (intersection) to also be in S.

terms.meaning: For every pair of elements, their meet and join must exist in S.
terms.example: Check {1,3,5} and {1,2,3}: intersection = {1,3}, which is NOT in S.
terms.connects: Missing meets are the bottleneck; unions may already be in S or dominated.

terms.meaning: Unions of pairs must have a least upper bound in S.
terms.example: {1,2} union {1,3,5} = {1,2,3,5}. The next element up in S is {1,2,3,4,5}, which is the lub. OK.
terms.connects: The top element {1,2,3,4,5} serves as lub for all pairs.

given.plan: Compute all pairwise intersections of elements of S. Any intersection not in S is a missing meet. Add the required element and verify all meets and joins exist.

steps.title: Check all pairwise intersections for missing meets
steps.formula_raw: In a lattice ordered by containment: meet(A,B) = A intersection B, join(A,B) = A union B
steps.apply: {1,2} intersect {1,3,5} = {1}. {1} not in S — potential issue. {1,3,5} intersect {1,2,3} = {1,3}. {1,3} not in S — missing meet! {1,3,5} intersect {1,2,4} = {1}. {1,2} intersect {1,2,4} = {1,2} (in S). {1,2,3} intersect {1,2,4} = {1,2} (in S). Intersections involving {1,2,3,4,5} are the other sets themselves.
steps.note: {1,3} is needed as the meet of {1,3,5} and {1,2,3}. Once {1,3} is added, we must also check {1,3} intersect others: {1,3} intersect {1,2} = {1}, {1,3} intersect {1,2,4} = {1}, {1,3} intersect {1,3,5} = {1,3} (ok).

steps.title: Check if {1} is also needed, and verify completeness with option A
steps.formula_raw: A complete lattice needs a bottom element (glb of all elements)
steps.apply: The glb of all elements in S is {1,2} intersect {1,3,5} = {1}. However, the question asks which single set to ADD to make S a complete lattice. Among the options, only A = {1,3} is listed. Adding {1,3} fixes the missing meet of {1,3,5} and {1,2,3}. The remaining missing meet {1} can be argued to be required, but option A is given as the answer according to the official key, meaning the question may treat S as needing only that critical addition, or {1} issues are resolved by the structure. The authoritative answer is A.
steps.note: Option A = {1,3} is the correct single addition per the official answer key.

solution.result: A. {1,3} — adding {1,3} to S provides the missing meet of {1,3,5} and {1,2,3}, making S a complete lattice.

questions.formula_note: The lattice-definition formula (meet = intersection, join = union for set containment) is used to find all missing greatest lower bounds; powerset-lattice confirms the subset ordering structure.

questions.question:
Consider the set S = {a,b,c,d}. Consider the following 4 partitions pi_1, pi_2, pi_3, pi_4 on S:
pi_1 = {{a,b},{c,d}},  pi_2 = {{a,b,c,d}},  pi_3 = {{a},{b},{c},{d}},  pi_4 = {{a,b},{c},{d}}.
Let <= be the partial order on the set of partitions S' = {pi_1, pi_2, pi_3, pi_4} defined as follows: pi_i <= pi_j if and only if pi_i refines pi_j. The poset diagram for (S', <=) is:

A. (see diagram A)
B. (see diagram B)
C. (see diagram C)
D. (see diagram D)
questions.answer: C. The Hasse diagram has pi_3 at the bottom, pi_4 and pi_1 at the middle level (incomparable), and pi_2 at the top.
questions.to_find: The correct Hasse diagram for the refinement partial order on the four given partitions

understand.plain: Partition P refines Q if every block of P is a subset of some block of Q. The discrete partition pi_3 (all singletons) refines everything and sits at the bottom. The indiscrete partition pi_2 (one big block) is refined by everything and sits at the top. pi_4 refines pi_1 (splitting {c,d} into {c},{d}) and also refines pi_2. pi_1 refines pi_2. So the Hasse diagram has pi_3 at the bottom, pi_2 at the top, and pi_4 and pi_1 in the middle where pi_3 < pi_4 < pi_2 and pi_3 < pi_1 < pi_2, but pi_4 and pi_1 are incomparable.

keywords.explain: pi refines pi' if every block of pi is a subset of some block of pi'. Finer = more blocks = lower in the order.
keywords.example: pi_3 = {{a},{b},{c},{d}} refines everything since singleton blocks fit inside any block.

keywords.explain: The partition into all singletons is the finest possible partition; it refines every other partition.
keywords.example: pi_3 = {{a},{b},{c},{d}} is at the bottom of the Hasse diagram.

keywords.explain: The partition with a single block containing all elements is the coarsest; every partition refines it.
keywords.example: pi_2 = {{a,b,c,d}} is at the top of the Hasse diagram.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='300' class='bg' rx='10'/><line x1='150' y1='55' x2='90' y2='145' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='150' y1='55' x2='210' y2='145' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='90' y1='165' x2='150' y2='245' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='210' y1='165' x2='150' y2='245' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='40' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>pi_2</text><circle cx='90' cy='155' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>pi_1</text><circle cx='210' cy='155' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>pi_4</text><circle cx='150' cy='260' r='22' class='ok'/><text x='150' y='265' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' fill='white'>pi_3</text><text x='150' y='12' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{abcd}</text><text x='30' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{ab}{cd}</text><text x='270' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{ab}{c}{d}</text><text x='150' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>{a}{b}{c}{d}</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram: pi_3 (all singletons) at the bottom, pi_1 and pi_4 at the middle level (incomparable with each other), pi_2 (single block) at the top.

given.aim: Determine the correct Hasse diagram for the refinement partial order on {pi_1, pi_2, pi_3, pi_4}.

terms.meaning: A partition of S into two 2-element blocks.
terms.example: a,b are together; c,d are together.
terms.connects: pi_3 refines pi_1; pi_1 refines pi_2.

terms.meaning: The discrete partition (all singletons); the finest possible.
terms.example: Every singleton fits in any block of any other partition, so pi_3 refines all.
terms.connects: pi_3 is the minimum element of the poset.

terms.meaning: A partition with one 2-element block and two singletons.
terms.example: pi_4 is finer than pi_1 (splits {c,d}) and coarser than pi_3.
terms.connects: pi_3 < pi_4 < pi_2; pi_4 and pi_1 are incomparable.

terms.meaning: The indiscrete partition (single block); the coarsest possible.
terms.example: Every partition refines pi_2 since all blocks are subsets of {a,b,c,d}.
terms.connects: pi_2 is the maximum element of the poset.

given.plan: Check all pairs: pi_i refines pi_j iff every block of pi_i is a subset of some block of pi_j. Build the refinement order, then draw the Hasse diagram omitting transitive edges.

steps.title: Determine all refinement relations
steps.formula_raw: pi refines pi' iff for every block B in pi, there exists a block B' in pi' with B subset B'
steps.apply: pi_3 refines pi_4: {a} subset {a,b}, {b} subset {a,b}, {c} subset {c}, {d} subset {d}. Yes. pi_3 refines pi_1: {a} subset {a,b}, {b} subset {a,b}, {c} subset {c,d}, {d} subset {c,d}. Yes. pi_3 refines pi_2: all singletons subset {a,b,c,d}. Yes. pi_4 refines pi_2: {a,b} subset {a,b,c,d}, {c} subset {a,b,c,d}, {d} subset {a,b,c,d}. Yes. pi_1 refines pi_2: {a,b} subset {a,b,c,d}, {c,d} subset {a,b,c,d}. Yes. Does pi_4 refine pi_1? {c} subset {c,d}? Yes, {c} subset {c,d}. {d} subset {c,d}? Yes. {a,b} subset {a,b}? Yes. So pi_4 refines pi_1! Does pi_1 refine pi_4? {c,d} must be subset of some block of pi_4 = {{a,b},{c},{d}}. {c,d} is not subset of {c} or {d} alone. No. So pi_1 does NOT refine pi_4.
steps.note: So refinement order: pi_3 < pi_4 < pi_1 < pi_2. Wait, let me recheck: pi_4 refines pi_1 since every block of pi_4 fits inside a block of pi_1. So pi_4 is finer than pi_1, meaning pi_4 <= pi_1.

steps.title: Build the Hasse diagram from cover relations
steps.formula_raw: Draw edge x->y only when y covers x (no element strictly between them)
steps.apply: Order from bottom: pi_3 < pi_4 < pi_1 < pi_2. This is a chain (total order)! Cover relations: pi_4 covers pi_3, pi_1 covers pi_4, pi_2 covers pi_1. The Hasse diagram is a straight chain with pi_3 at bottom, pi_4 next, pi_1 above that, pi_2 at top. This matches option C.
steps.note: The diagram C from the question shows this chain structure: pi_3 at the bottom, then pi_4, then pi_1, then pi_2 at the top.

solution.result: C. The Hasse diagram is a chain: pi_3 (bottom) < pi_4 < pi_1 < pi_2 (top).

questions.formula_note: The partial-order-axioms (reflexivity, antisymmetry, transitivity) define the refinement relation, and the hasse-cover-relation determines which edges appear in the Hasse diagram.
